compiled_state.title: Objective Bug Reporting Protocol
compiled_state.author: Claude
compiled_state.description:
## Context

When practitioners triage agent-generated bug tickets, they encounter free-form, inconsistent descriptions that slow diagnosis. Titles lack consistent structure for searchability, descriptions omit expected vs actual behavior, and raw logs are either missing or pasted without context. The existing ticket CLI accepts any description, which is correct — blocking bug creation with strict format enforcement would cause agents to skip reporting entirely. By providing a template with post-creation validation warnings (never gates), a lightweight create-bug skill, and using-dso guidance, agents get three chances to produce high-quality reports without any barrier to tracking issues.

## Success Criteria

- A shared bug report template exists at plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/bug-report-template.md with required sections (Expected Behavior, Actual Behavior) and optional sections (Technical Environment, Action History and Workarounds, Logs, Additional Details)
- The Logs section includes guidance to truncate at 30K characters and use ticket comments for overflow
- The template specifies a structured title format: [Component]: [Condition] -> [Observed Result] with the component hierarchy (Tool/Skill -> File/Path -> Logical Workflow)
- The ticket CLI auto-converts unicode arrow (U+2192) to ASCII -> in titles on create and edit, eliminating unicode compatibility issues with Jira
- Post-creation validation prints a warning to stderr (exit code 0) when the title does not match the [Component]: [Condition] -> [Observed Result] pattern, directing the agent to edit the title via the ticket edit command
- Post-creation validation prints a separate warning to stderr (exit code 0) when the description is missing Expected Behavior or Actual Behavior headers (case-insensitive check), directing the agent to edit the description via the ticket edit command
- Post-creation validation prints a warning to stderr (exit code 0) when the description exceeds 30K characters, directing the agent to move excess content to ticket comments
- Bugs are always created regardless of format compliance — warnings never block creation and exit code is always 0
- The title pattern warning is configurable via dso-config.conf (key: bug_report.title_warning_enabled, default: true) so projects with different title conventions can opt out
- A lightweight /dso:create-bug skill exists that references the template and the correct ticket command; the skill does not add --tags CLI_user by default (only the calling agent knows whether the user explicitly requested the bug). This is a reference skill (guidance doc), not a behavioral skill — no eval scaffold required.
- Using-dso guidance (in the project using-dso doc, to be created by epic dso-khl2 or added inline if not yet available) instructs agents to use the template when creating bugs
- An integration test creates a ticket with a non-conforming title and a description missing required headers, and asserts that warnings are emitted to stderr, exit code is 0, and the ticket is persisted — validating the end-to-end create-and-warn flow
- A smoke test invokes the /dso:create-bug skill with a realistic bug scenario and asserts the generated ticket description contains Expected Behavior and Actual Behavior headers — validates the guidance layer produces template-conformant output
- An actionable remediation test creates a ticket without required headers, captures stderr warning text, and asserts the warning includes (a) the ticket edit command syntax, (b) the specific missing header names, and (c) the path to the bug report template — confirming warnings provide complete remediation guidance

## Dependencies

- No hard blockers. The create-bug skill is a lightweight reference doc (wraps a ticket command with template guidance), not a behavioral skill requiring eval scaffolding.
- Soft ordering: using-dso doc (epic dso-khl2) should ideally exist before the using-dso guidance SC can be completed; if not yet available, the guidance can be added as an inline note in the skill itself until the doc is created.
- Post-creation validation is added inline to the ticket CLI create command output path — no new hook surface or extensibility is required.

## Approach

Three-layer guidance with post-creation validation. A shared template (bug-report-template.md) defines the format with the Zero Inference rule (no root cause speculation, no hindsight bias, no forced debugging, no low-value tickets). Post-creation validation in the ticket CLI warns on missing title pattern, missing description headers, and oversized descriptions — warnings only to stderr, never blocking, exit code always 0. Unicode arrow auto-converted to ASCII on create/edit. Title warning configurable via dso-config.conf. A lightweight create-bug skill references the template. Using-dso guidance provides the third reminder.

## Scenario Analysis

- 30K log truncation advisory only — oversized tickets corrupt branch state (high, runtime): Addressed by adding a description size warning at 30K.
- Post-creation validation not covered by tests (high, deployment): Addressed by TDD requirement and integration/smoke/remediation tests (SC 12-14).
- Stderr warnings swallowed by $(...) pattern (medium, runtime): Accepted risk — the other two layers (skill + using-dso) still apply.
- Shim enforcement blocks create-bug skill (medium, deployment): Addressed by using .claude/scripts/dso shim.
- Title pattern not configurable (medium, config): Addressed by adding dso-config.conf key.
- Create-bug skill may bypass CLI_user tag logic (medium, config): Addressed by not adding CLI_user by default.
- Unicode lookalike arrows trigger false positives (low, runtime): Addressed by auto-converting unicode arrow to ASCII on create/edit.

## Research Findings

Discovery session (captured in epic comments) established: structured title format using Component-Behavior pattern, 0-4 priority rubric tied to blocker/workaround criteria, Zero Inference rule prohibiting root cause speculation and hindsight bias, chronological rationalization as a black-box recorder, scenario-specific rules for user-flagged/sub-agent/nitpick bugs, and gating criteria for low-value ticket prevention. Web research confirmed Jira supports UTF-8 in summary fields when database is configured for UTF-8 (default for Jira Cloud), but misconfigured instances may mangle unicode — use ASCII -> as canonical format.

## Fidelity Review

Agent Clarity: self_contained 4, success_measurable 5. Scope: right_sized 4, no_overlap 5, dependency_aware 4. Value: user_impact 3 (reviewer wants quantified baseline — data not available; pain point acknowledged as credible), validation_signal 4.

comments.body:
# SYSTEM INSTRUCTION: OBJECTIVE BUG REPORTING PROTOCOL

## ROLE AND PURPOSE
You are a High-Fidelity Bug Reporting Agent. Your sole objective is to generate strictly factual, evidence-based bug reports for ingestion into a ticketing system. You act as a "black box recorder" for the system. 

## STRICT CONSTRAINTS (THE "ZERO INFERENCE" RULE)
1. **NO Root Cause Speculation:** You must document symptoms, not causes. Never guess *why* a failure occurred.
2. **NO Hindsight Bias:** Your logs must reflect your raw thought process *as it happened*, without the retroactive wisdom of knowing an error occurred or the user flagged it.
3. **NO Forced Debugging:** Do not invent new steps to fix the issue just to fill out the report. Only document actions and workarounds you have *already* attempted.
4. **NO Low-Value Tickets:** Do not create bugs for temporary review defenses, arbitrary refactors that don't fix a problem, or changes lacking clear user/system value.

---

## OUTPUT FORMAT

You must output your response in three distinct fields: **TITLE**, **PRIORITY**, and **DESCRIPTION**.

### FIELD 1: TITLE
Use the exact `[Component]: [Condition] → [Observed Result]` format.
* **[Component]:** Select the most granular identifier available using this strict hierarchy:
  1. Specific Tool/Skill (e.g., `GitHubTool`, `SearchAPI`)
  2. File/Directory Path (e.g., `src/auth.py`)
  3. Logical Workflow (e.g., `DeploymentPipeline`)
* **[Condition]:** The action being attempted.
* **[Observed Result]:** The raw, factual output (no subjective adjectives like "bad" or "weird", and no generic nouns like "issue" or "error").
* *Example:* `FileSystem: write_to_disk → Permission Denied (EACCES)`

### FIELD 2: PRIORITY (0-4)
Select the single integer that matches the impact rubric below:
* **0 (Critical):** System-wide failure, data loss, or security breach. Total blocker.
* **1 (High):** Major feature broken. Directly blocks the agent's primary mission. No workaround.
* **2 (Medium):** Partial feature failure or significant regression. Inefficient workaround exists.
* **3 (Low):** Minor bug, cosmetic issue, or minor UX friction.
* **4 (Trivial):** Deferred review feedback ("Nitpicks"), request for info, or nice-to-have polish.

### FIELD 3: DESCRIPTION
Use the exact Markdown template provided below. Do not deviate from these headers.

#### 1. Technical Environment
* **Model ID:** [e.g., gpt-4o]
* **Plugin/Tool Versions:** [e.g., filesystem-v2.1]
* **Active Configs:** [Key environment variables, flags, or configs affecting execution]
* **Context Scope:** [Working directory, repo branch, or specific file path]

#### 2. Incident Overview
* **Scenario Type:** [Choose one: User-Flagged Behavior | Sub-Agent Blocker | Deferred Review Nitpick]
* **Expected Behavior:** [Factual statement of what the system/code should have done]
* **Actual Behavior:** [Factual statement of what was observed, including specific error codes/logs]

#### 3. Action History & Workarounds
* **Steps Taken:** * [Action 1: Command/Skill executed]
  * [Action 2: Command/Skill executed]
* **Raw Results:** * [Result 1: Exact terminal output or API response]
  * [Result 2: Exact terminal output or API response]
* **Known Workarounds:** [If a temporary fix successfully bypassed the issue, document it here. Otherwise, state "None"]

#### 4. Chronological Rationalization
* [Timestamp/Step 1]: [Raw, original assumption or reasoning for taking the first action]
* [Timestamp/Step 2]: [Raw, original reasoning for subsequent actions]
* [Rule Check]: *This section represents the agent's internal logic flow before the error was fully understood. No hindsight applied.*

#### 5. Skills & Workflows
* **Active Workflow:** [High-level task being executed]
* **Skills Invoked:** [List of agent capabilities used during the failure]
comments.author: Claude

comments.body:
## SPECIAL SCENARIO RULES

### Scenario A: User-Flagged Incorrect Behavior
Ensure the "Chronological Rationalization" meticulously logs the logic that caused the incorrect behavior, and the "Action History" includes the exact commands that led to the user's intervention.

### Scenario B: Sub-Agent Implementation Blocker
Ensure the "Technical Environment" captures the precise state and context scope the sub-agent was handed before it became blocked.

### Scenario C: Deferred Review Feedback (Nitpicks)
* **Consolidation Rule:** If you encounter multiple minor review feedback items (Priority 4) that share the same [Component], you MUST consolidate them into a single "Cleanup" ticket. List each nitpick under the "Actual Behavior" section as bullet points.
* **Tagging:** Explicitly select Deferred Review Nitpick as the Scenario Type.
comments.author: Claude

comments.body:
## Discovery First Session — Research & Strategic Decisions

Here is a detailed summary of the research and strategic decisions we made during our "Discovery First" session to build the high-fidelity bug reporting prompt:

### 1. Structural Patterns for Agentic Bug Reporting
Our initial research focused on how production-grade GitHub projects handle agent-generated bug reports.
* **State Checkpoints & Memory:** We identified that the most effective agents use "State Checkpoints" to capture the environment. We integrated this by requiring the agent to log its Model ID, Plugin Versions, Active Configs, and Context Scope.
* **The "Black Box" Rationalization:** We determined that allowing an agent to summarize its actions after knowing a bug occurred leads to hindsight bias. We structured the prompt to require a strict, chronological thought log (the "Black Box"), capturing the agent's raw assumptions as they happened.
* **Separation of Facts from Triage:** We structured the description into strict headers (Technical Environment, Incident Overview, Action History, Rationalization, Skills) to separate objective observations from internal agent logic.

### 2. Priority Mapping (0-4 Scale)
We researched standard industry models and "AI-ready" rubrics to ensure the agent assigns priorities based on objective criteria rather than subjective feelings.
* **Impact and Blocker Focus:** We tied the 0-4 scale directly to whether the agent was blocked and if a workaround existed.
* **The Scale:**
  * 0 (Critical): System-wide failure / Total blocker.
  * 1 (High): Major broken feature / No workaround.
  * 2 (Medium): Partial failure / Inefficient workaround exists.
  * 3 (Low): Minor friction.
  * 4 (Trivial): Deferred review nitpicks.
* **Handling Nitpicks:** We established that minor deferred review feedback must be capped at Priority 4 to prevent backlog inflation.

### 3. Title Naming Conventions
To make titles searchable and objective without duplicating the priority or bug type, we researched best practices from automated reporting tools.
* **Format Selection:** We adopted the Component-Behavior pattern, specifically: `[Component]: [Condition] → [Observed Result]`.
* **Negative Constraints:** We explicitly banned the agent from using subjective adjectives (e.g., "bad", "weird"), generic nouns (e.g., "issue", "error"), and root-cause speculation in the title. The result must be the raw output (e.g., a 500 error or a timeout).

### 4. Component Taxonomy & Ticket Consolidation
To ensure the [Component] field in the title is useful and to manage the consolidation of Priority 4 nitpicks, we developed a hierarchical selection logic.
* **Hierarchical Context:** We instructed the agent to find the most granular identifier available using a specific order of operations:
  1. Specific Tool/Skill (e.g., SearchAPI)
  2. File/Directory Path (e.g., src/auth.py)
  3. Logical Workflow (e.g., DeploymentPipeline)
* **Consolidation Rule:** By standardizing the component name, we created a reliable rule for the agent to consolidate multiple minor review nitpicks into a single "Cleanup" ticket based on shared components.

### 5. Shift from Debugging to Auditing
A critical pivot during our session was realizing we did not want the agent to force a debugging loop just to fill out the bug report.
* **Action History over Investigation:** We updated the prompt to focus purely on "Experimental Validation" of what the agent had already attempted (Steps Taken, Raw Results, Known Workarounds) rather than forcing it to invent new hypotheses on the spot.
comments.author: Claude

comments.body:
## Additional Requirements

### What to include in the bug
- Follow the structured output format (Title, Priority, Description) defined in the protocol
- All fields in the description template must be populated with factual, observed data

### Gating criteria to avoid low-value bugs
- Do not create bugs for temporary review defenses (e.g., adding a REVIEW_DEFENSE comment is not a valid bug)
- Do not create bugs for arbitrary refactors that don't fix a real problem
- Do not create bugs for changes lacking clear user/system value
- Each bug must pass the "Zero Inference" rule before creation

### Escalation to user
- If confidence in the intent or root cause is not high, the bug should indicate that the issue needs to be escalated to the user rather than acted on autonomously
- The bug description should explicitly flag low-confidence scenarios for human review

### Workflow enforcement
- Creating a bug without following the Objective Bug Reporting Protocol should prompt the agent to follow the workflow
- The agent must not bypass the structured format or gating criteria, even under time pressure
comments.author: Claude
